What's the difference between capitalism and Capitalism? Are you just describing the act of exchanging resources as capitalism? The Soviet Union fed its workers (usually lol), was that exchange of resources "capitalism?"This isn't a really accurate use of terms, and imo is an example of the hypernormalization of capitalism, or as the op said, reification. There's a tendency in people to believe that things that existed as recently as a day before they were born, have existed for essentially forever, and are as certain as gravity. In reality, capitalism, which is the private ownership of the means of production in service of accumulating capital, is as stated very young. We have about 25,000 years of human society operating without capitalism.Trade is not capitalism, nor did the switch to agrarian society represent a capitalist choice. It's frankly a bog standard example of primitive communism. Fields were owned in common by a society, worked in common, reaped in common, consumed in common. Depending on the society, you might have elders, matriarchs, patriarchs etc that determined how resources would be allocated (e.g. successful hunters getting more, spiritual leaders getting more, assholes getting less), but those people weren't "owners" by our capitalist understanding. Nobody paid them rent.Furthermore, one village trading with another e.g. ox for squash or whatever, isn't capitalism. Even if the ox were "privately owned," and exchanged for e.g. a dowry, the land on which the ox feeds is still owned in common, and such resources were still generally expected to be shared in emergency or for religious or cultural events. Depending on the society of course, but it's a pretty common thread throughout history - the gift economy, and primitive communism.I think it's very important to keep clear on terms and our history, because dogmatism can cast our feet in concrete and prevent us from trying new, exciting things that are possibly more well suited for the times. One could argue that the global takeover by capitalism is evidence of its effectiveness in all aspects of organizing society, I would say it's more likely to be evidence at its effectiveness of spreading and consuming societies while regulating them just enough to prevent their collapse. Efficient? Hardly - look at the wealth disparity between Apple shareholders and the factory workers that throw themselves off buildings after making the phones that generate apple's fat profit margins. How is that efficient? Therefore I want to push back on this idea of capitalism as gravity to leave us room to explore new ideas.

I think understanding and exploring the universe is an essential "success metric" for intelligent life like humanity -- but I don't think it's at the expense of all else. I mentioned it because it, to me, makes a humanity that abandoned complexity a "failed" humanity. Although again, on an individual basis I think this is a fine option.An underlying principle I believe in is an avoidance of waste. It's this principle that underpins part of why I think there is an obligation for humanity to understand/explore: to avoid wasting our improbable "gift". This principle constrains the principle of understanding/exploration and relates to Earth. Earth and life on Earth is itself rare and the result of its own biological lotteries. To blindly exploit Earth's resources is not only wasteful but shortsighted as well towards humanity's own survival. So I think I'm in stewardship on that spectrum, but need to sit with it a bit more.With regards to the first article, I think it outlines many of the complexities around humanity's space travel and habitation. For me, the key bit is understanding and exploration; ie the seeing/understanding of what the universe is/has (on Earth as well as elsewhere). I don't actually think this has to be humanity. I think more broadly the obligation I've mentioned lies with intelligent life not necessarily humanity (we just happen to be the only example of such we're aware of). Habitation isn't as big a piece for me. If we can send robotic "eyes" for intelligence to see through, or if we create other intelligent life with different properties from humanity that can see/explore, I consider this goal met.

As I see it, the quote neither advocates nor critiques unreasonableness, but rather observes that unreasonable people are most often the ones responsible for change. Whether you take that as a lesson on the merits of unreasonableness, the dangers of it, or something in between, is up to interpretation, and depends on how much one values reasonableness vs progress (for the record, I've heard the quote more often in a negative sense by people who put reasonableness above "progress"). It also depends on one's definition of "reasonableness" of course, and whether something can be unreasonable yet still a positive.So I guess my point is that the quote can mean just about whatever you want it to mean. It's an interesting litmus test. I do agree that people using it as carte blanche for unreasonableness in the name of some sort of nebulous "progress" is, well, unreasonable, though with context I'm certain GP was using it as more of a critique.

Nature is indifferent. One year may produce an overabundance that the hunter/gatherer may take advantage of, yet the next year may be opposite and people will die from famine. So we learned how to preserve food as best we could. Yet that would result in a growth of population, an over population based on the resources available, so we learned how to grow our own food and manage livestock in order to avoid famine. That encourages the development of settlements. With denser populations disease is able to thrive, and, with trade, it is able to spread. So we learned how to manage waste. Each new development brings new pitfalls since we are meddling with the balance of nature. Or perhaps it is better to say that things are being balanced in new ways, so we must learn how to adapt to that. (We are, after all, a part of nature.)Sometimes we adapt to those changes in balance in ignorant and extraordinarily damaging ways. I am not denying that. On the other hand, not trying would have hindered the development of intelligence -- or, perhaps, resulted in our extinction.

Mostly I agree with overall perspective and tenor of the piece, but there's a profound absence of (historical) awareness, paired with a weird, presumptuous, sophomoric sanctimoniousness -- clearest in the strange insistence on using the word "we." If you've ever listened to recordings of sermons from Jamestown, you'll hear something similar: the breathless outrage and stupefaction at what "we" have become and what "we" do and "the world today." It's millenarianism and apocalypticism, and it's just goofy. It's the tone of a kid in his mid-teens who is worked up by his latest epiphany: he finally gets it and is wildly excited to make it clear, and he's performing it and acting it out for his parents, showing how serious he is -- and all the adults in the room know that he's on his way to figuring something out but doesn't grasp that he's trying on an idea and a personality to see how it feels. I hear the same cluelessness in this piece.

Malta has a population of 500k. Let's assume 100k people use MaltaGPT daily, and they send an average of 10 messages per day, so roughly 1M messages per day. That averages 694 per minute, but at peak could be 3-5x that, so let's say 3000 per minute. Usage will of course vary by day of week and time of day (they could partner with a Pacific island and share inference hardware).Those 3000 messages per minute translate to 50 messages per second. Let's say average prompt input is 5k tokens, and output is 500. So 250k tokens per second for prompt processing (let's ignore caching for simplicity) and 25k tokens per second for output decode.If we take a 500B dense model, that concerts to roughly 1 trillion flops per token. So we need 250 petaflops per second of prompt processing and 25 petaflops for output decode. So 275 PFLOPS in compute.That may sound like a lot, however a NVIDIA DGX B200 machine (8xB200) has a compute of 144 PFLOPS at FP4. That is assuming 100% efficiency which isn't really possible, and we also need to factor in memory usage which we would be limited more by than compute. So let's say we'd need 10 of them. For an entire country to have a sovereign version of ChatGPT.The cloud cost to rent one machine is around $50/hour, so that would mean our cluster comes to $4.8m per year. However the list price of a machine is around €400k, so the price to buy the cluster outright would be around €5m (you need the rest of the data center too), with operating costs of around €500k per year.So per citizen: €10 upfront and €1 per year.

For other readers who may be too young to remember, improper privacy controls (unenforced HTTPS, poor encryption in the form of WEP, easy MitM attacks, etc) meant that public/untrusted WiFi was a legitimate security risk as things like passwords, bank details, etc were very easy to steal as they were sent unencrypted over the air. This is fortunately much less true these days with the advent of better protections across the entire stack (HTTPS everywhere, WPA*, etc) but unscrupulous VPN merchants still use this outdated argument to try to sell their products to less technically-savvy customers.What these technologies (and VPNs) _do not_ prevent is the legitimate (and consensual) capture of user data by captive portal software (email, phone, etc), which is typically submitted by a user wishing to connect to a public network. This is what the parent comment is mentioning. Different risk profiles, obviously.

If middle-actors can't snoop, then can logs on both ends positively identify the other party? Was there a common way pre-internet, where people anonymously gathered and discussed things, with capability to harm each other, but without the authorities being able to do anything about it after the fact?If the authorities are able to gain access to a private key, or some other proof of possession of one end of the connection, can the VPN provider, the network, or the protocol disclose the identity of the source of traffic on the other end?I'm only making these arguments to point out how nuanced the topic is. The problem is precisely that it doesn't show that. The Online Safety Act is, on this public explainer, described as legislation that provides protections to multiple groups. What they say in paragraph two is that "the strongest protections" are offered to children, while paragraph three then calls out that "The act will also protect adult users".What is described is a tiered set of protections that at its lowest protects everyone (including adults), and a set of more narrow protections that are only extended to children. It follows quite logically that you will only need to know the users age if you want to show content to adults that you are not allowed to show children.The "categorization" they are discussing is another axis of "tiering". Smaller provides (in categories 2A and B) are imposed less duty of protection, according to the explainer to account for their "size and capacity".With this context. I think it's quite clear that the comments about the targeting of Category 1 are completely pedestrian. It isn't supposed to apply differently to PornHub and Amazon, because both are large multinationals that have enough resources to uphold their imposed duty.For this to reveal anything nefarious about age verification, it would have to be about the designations of "Primary Priority Content" and "Priority Content" which are the types of content you are allowed to show adults, but not children.It is all intensely boring, so I can't blame the news from not wanting to cover it, but it is exactly the type of context you have to include when making quite extraordinary accusations of misleading the public.

What makes problems into disasters is denying that there is a problem until it is too late.Past generations mostly tried (with varying success) to fix the problems in their world. Sometimes the past generations' solutions are good, like much of the world mandating 40 hour work weeks and public pensions and workplace health and safety and so on; other times even when the problem is real, the solutions are worse, like the US experience with prohibition.But when problems get ignored, you get stuff like leaded gasoline, cigarettes, and asbestos being everywhere, the Irish potato famine, the dissolution of the USSR, and the 2007 global financial crisis.Even if AI doesn't do what it promises, the internet brings with it even more globalisation, cheap labour that undercuts any rich nation for jobs which can be done on a computer (which we've already seen examples of, not just with coding but also call centres). And the internet does enable much weirder warfare: our governments can blame hacks on whoever they like, but there is often no dramatic photo of something burning as a result, just a diffuse degradation of economic performance from fully automated scams and blackmails.And that's without any questions about demographic shift and who pays for the current generation's pensions when they retire, and if this has anything to do with free porn and the state of online dating apps. And without personalised propaganda. Without your home surveillance system (or robot vacuum cleaner) being turned against you by hacks only possible from cheap ubiquitous internet. Without any questions about if doomscrolling does or doesn't induce psychological problems, if sexual deepfakes are worse than schoolyard rumours, or if AI is sopping kids from learning as cheating is easier.

Practically speaking, when I look at the actual number of people affected by VPN I estimate that:  - Very low: Protecting political activists and dissidents
  - Low: Circumvention of overzealous blocking and surveillance
  - Low-to-Medium: Hiding abusive and malicious behavior
  - Medium: Additional layers of trust and network security (mostly business related, which makes it tangental to the consumer VPN market)
  - VERY High: Enabling piracy and avoiding geo-content restrictions (no judgment on good-vs-bad, just asserting magnitude)

I believe that management at VPN companies are extremely pro-consumer protection (if only because their cash flows depend on this). I absolutely trust the system and network administrators. They don't want to track or look at the data flows because the odds of seeing something nasty is quite high. I have a fair amount of professional industry experience to back this up.So... conundrum! If I take the position that piracy-related stuff isn't a net drag and that business VPN use is fundamentally a separate beast, VPNs in this context are hard to justify.

I think they become less fun sometimes during the later stage Web 2.0 era as everything shifted toward services. For most, computers became a thin client to access services on the Internet. To me, this isn't very fun. The computer becomes an appliance to access services rather than one used to run programs that can be customized and tailored to the user. Of course that local experience can still exist, but only for users who are very intentional. For shared experiences, it's only for users whose friends are also intentional. This intentionality usually means doing a lot of extra work. This work was not an issue when the pay off of new functionality was there, but is harder to justify when it's often less functionality for more work… the main upside being freedom and agency over the experience.Seemingly every service being a front to collect and sell data also makes things less fun. There is a certain level of trust that needs to exist when running software and that trust relationship has been broken, even by real companies. It makes it hard to trust anything, which pushes me away from trying new things that might be fun.This change coincided with my transition to adulthood, where priorities shift. It's impossible to say if I'd still find computers fun in 2026 if I was 19 again, but my gut tells me I'd be a lot less interested.If there is a path back, I think it might be along the lines of Pewdiepie's newfound love of Linux. He seems to be having a ball.

Earlier computers, and particularly the early Internet, were all about personal freedom. The computer served you and the Internet was there to connect you with peers. So that was fun and the possibilities were literally infinite.As the giant corporations and particularly the advertising industry (which destroys everything it touches) have slowly taken control, computers and the Internet no longer serve us, the people. We are now the product, to be fully monetized at every stage. Monetization alone is bad but not terrible, but in order to maximize monetization the corporations also need to exert control, thus all the increasingly restrictive technology where we are very clearly only a paying consumer kept within a box, not a participant.While the technology world has been almost entirely enshittified, it's good to keep some optimism though. The fun parts still exist, they're just more effort to find.On the Internet, support your hobby email lists (open participation, no corporate control), support enthusiast-driven forums and websites (independently run, no social media giants monetizing every message!). There are fewer of these but they do exist, support them. Or start your own! Don't let the megacorporations own your hobby interactions.On the programming side, work on something hands-on fun if you like. When I want to geek out programming like it's 1995, I have a BSD box and work on a hobby project which is all in C. No vibe coding, no thousands of unknown npm dependencies, none of that junk. Just me and emacs and the kernel and the compiler. I don't work on it often but every now and then a weekend of real programming is very relaxing and reminds me how much fun it is.

> Any suggestion on getting it back?1. Take a break. I don't mean stop working, just fill your free time with other stuff, but if you don't code at work, it is a bonus. Try other things. I was in a position you describe, and I took a break, dived into studying psychology, you can choose anything you like, like keeping stray cat population in your neighborhood fed and sufficiently stroked behind their ears. Or can you play a harp? You can learn it, you know. (Speaking about psychology: your condition is a middle age crisis. Self-realization is done, all immediate goals are reached, you've got more freedom to choose next goals, and everything is increasingly becoming a routine. Nothing bad is happening, you just need to adapt. You will adapt, you don't need to do anything special, it will happen all by itself. Just exercise your new freedom a little, and in a couple of years new goals will find you.)2. Stop thinking of important things. Had you read "Surely you are joking, Mr. Feynman!"? If not, I suggest to read just for fun of it, but among all the jokes Feynman describes a period in his life when he felt like you. He couldn't find any good ideas what he could do in physics. At some point he took a seemingly silly problem, and solved it. And he came back to physics with it, later he discovered the differential equations from that silly problem in another "serious" problem and eventually got a Nobel Prize.The moral is: you are overthinking it. You are looking for ideas for programs but you use too narrow filters. Like the problem should match your skills. Or the problem should be important enough to work on it.So just take a break and look for some interesting problem. If you find any, you find some other way to entertain yourself, so not a big deal. Just stop obsessing about it and I'm sure it will come naturally.

To me a more important question is: where can people 10-30 years old bootstrap themselves on interesting and useful problems?  They have intelligence and energy but not resources or connections (mostly), and all that potential human capital will all be wasted if they don't have any tractable and fruitful domains.  (We don't have to worry about those with resources, connections, or luck, but they're a small minority already tethered to value flows, in little danger of being under-developed.)My concern in particular is that tech companies spent big on building free languages and tools (yes, you used to have to pay for compilers, IDE's, databases...) in order to reduce input costs of them and their customers.  If AI already minimizes labor costs (both the work and the discovery and training of residual human talent), there's no reason to subsidize that self-training, and likely fewer portable skills (across more isolated tech stacks), further locking employees in, reducing cross-pollination (formerly within the valley).Individual opportunity is shrinking.  Young people feel it.  Old people feel it too, even if they have bagfuls of tech stocks.

Linux kep it fun.  Even during a time when I worked with windows professionally I always had a Linux distribution installed somewhere.As a short history of fun in no particular order:First time I got XFree86 working after having to endlessly configure display settings.Using Yggdrasil to do remote support for Sun systems.Hacking on a cd-rom driver to get mine to work.Building a media server on an NSLU2.  My media server is still called “the slug” despite being on an RPi now.  At one point my kids had repurposed PS2s in their rooms with all their favourite shows at their fingertips.  Sounds lame now but back then it was magic.Moving all my dev tools to Linux after finally realising they all ran worse on windows, including .NET coreEndless fiddling with wine to get games to start, now completely solved but it was educational at the time.Wacky shenanigans with wifi drivers and binary blobs back when wifi was still emerging.I don't know how you get the spark back once it's lost though, I'd only suggest that the reward centre in your dome doesn't fire properly unless you've been challenged and worked hard to solve whatever you're faced with.

Title: Hidradenitis Suppurativa Tied to Higher Cardiovascular Risks
Description:
Edited by Deepa Varma

Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is associated with an increased risk for cardiovascular comorbidities in a meta-analysis, with hypertension affecting approximately one quarter of patients.

The study “reinforces the emerging view of HS as a systemic inflammatory disease with potential cardiovascular implications and supports further investigation into the contribution of systemic inflammation to cardiovascular risk,” the authors of the study wrote. The results, they added, “support consideration of routine cardiovascular risk assessment and management of modifiable cardiometabolic risk factors as part of comprehensive HS care.”

The study was led by Sophia A. Mense, Rush University Medical College, Chicago, and was published online on May 11 in the British Journal of Dermatology.

Limitations included the observational design of included studies, limiting causal inference and high heterogeneity.

The study received no specific funding. One author disclosed serving as an advisor, consultant, speaker, and/or investigator for multiple pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies including AbbVie; Acelyrin Inc.; Alumis Inc.; Amgen, and AnaptysBio, Inc.

Title: From Dr Google to AI: Are Health Searches Fueling Mistrust?
Description:
COMMENTARY

Elena Chanassery Poykkattu

Medical student (third year), University of Valladolid, Spain

Disclosure: Elena Chanassery Poykkattu has disclosed no relevant financial relationships.

Medical student (third year), University of Valladolid, Spain

Disclosure: Elena Chanassery Poykkattu has disclosed no relevant financial relationships.

As medical students, we find ourselves at a unique crossroads: We are digital natives who understand the craving for immediate information, yet we are also future physicians who recognize the risks of oversimplifying clinical matters.

This duality helps us to serve as a bridge between the healthcare system and a population that, although more informed than ever, remains caught in the paradox of information overload. Easy access to data has not translated into greater health literacy, but rather into increased uncertainty and clinical mistrust.

This trend began with “Dr Google” and has since evolved into use of generative AI tools, such as ChatGPT, alongside the rise of health influencers on social media. Although these are different tools that coexist today, both produce a shared side effect: They are reshaping medical consultations.

As interns working with primary care physicians, we increasingly meet patients with high levels of anxiety who are convinced they have serious conditions based solely on an online search. For instance, they may believe they've had a stroke when they actually have a tension headache.

The risk lies in the perception of the accuracy that these models convey. Medicine, however, is a science built not on isolated data points, but rather on clinical judgment.

No advanced algorithm, however, can replicate the human ability to reason — one shaped by rigorous training and clinical experience, which takes into account a patient's medical history, physical examination, and socioeconomic context.

Medical training teaches us that a therapeutic approach must account for everything from the socioeconomic context to the single-nucleotide polymorphisms that determine how a specific patient metabolizes a drug. Delegating such decisions to an algorithm is a step backward from precision medicine to one-size-fits-all medicine.

Content such as Instagram Reels that promise quick fixes for acne or weight loss through supplements reinforces a superficial view of health by focusing only on symptoms rather than underlying disease processes. Scientific medicine, in contrast, does not look for cosmetic or temporary relief; it seeks to resolve the underlying pathophysiologic causes, something a commercial recommendation algorithm will never be equipped to prioritize.

A prime example is the way in which polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS), formerly known as polycystic ovary syndrome, is discussed on social media.

It is common to find success stories built around heavy supplement use to treat acne or hirsutism — stories that overlook the fact that medicine targets not cosmetic relief but the underlying cause.

Adopting such treatments without a differential diagnosis is a critical error. A protocol designed for insulin-resistant PMOS will not be effective for adrenal or inflammatory conditions. Moreover, those Instagram “success stories” often obscure the fact that the supplement is merely an adjunct to a regulated medical treatment that goes unmentioned, thereby misleading the reader or viewer.

However, this is not a hypothetical scenario. Now that information is readily available, a worrying trend toward self-diagnosis and unsupervised self-medication has taken hold.

It is essential to understand that turning to AI for answers does not stem from recklessness but from a patient's natural anxiety about their illness. The risk arises when digital consultation becomes a barrier in the doctor-patient relationship, fueling confrontation rooted in confirmation bias. For this very reason, 21st-century healthcare professionals must not only diagnose and treat but also guide patients toward reliable sources of information.

The trend toward self-diagnosis and mistrust of healthcare professionals should therefore be met not with criticism but with support. Healthcare professionals are not infallible but have the judgment to discern and filter evidence. The medical encounter extends far beyond a diagnosis; it involves empathy, an understanding of the patient's context, and a holistic approach — something neither a stranger behind a screen nor an algorithm can provide.

We must remember that even when two patients share the same diagnosis, their biological and psychosocial individuality means that they will never present the same clinical picture, etiology, or therapeutic response. AI classifies based on patterns and physician reasons based on individuality. To treat the patient — not just the disease — and to move beyond the generic approach offered by the Internet, we must understand the person in their entirety.

The challenge faced by our generation is to pursue a holistic approach. In the face of algorithmic reductionism, our response must be clinical empathy and a thorough understanding of pathophysiology.

Technology should serve as a supportive tool — never as a substitute for a doctor-patient relationship grounded in trust and scientific rigor.

Title: Acute Kidney Injury Sets Kids Up for Chronic Kidney Disease
Description:
Edited by Archita Rai

Children who survived acute kidney injury (AKI) showed increased long-term risks for chronic kidney disease (CKD) and death, according to a meta-analysis.

“Integrating AKI follow-up into existing pediatric chronic disease management frameworks may facilitate the earlier identification of CKD and timely intervention. Additionally, empowering caregivers and patients with preventive strategies, such as optimizing blood pressure control, avoiding nephrotoxic exposure, and promoting healthy lifestyle habits, may mitigate long-term complications,” the researchers wrote.

The study was led by Jitendra Meena, MD, DM, of All India Institute of Medical Sciences in New Delhi, India. It was published online on May 4 in JAMA Pediatrics.

Quality of studies varied and several key outcomes were reported in only a small number of studies.

The authors did not report any funding source or conflicts of interest.

Title: Lupus Can Cost Young Women More Years Than Diabetes, HIV
Description:
Edited by Javed Choudhury

A population-based study showed that lupus accounted for roughly 304,200 years of potential life lost among US women aged 15-44 years (2000-2015), ranking 14th among the leading causes of premature death in that age group and ninth among women aged 15-24 years, surpassing causes such as diabetes and HIV infection.

“The [years of potential life lost] ranking gives a new and meaningful perspective regarding premature mortality in lupus and supplements other methods of assessing disease burden. Recognition of lupus as a leading contributor to premature mortality may inform public health reporting, prevention priorities, and research prioritization,” the authors of the study wrote.

The study was led by Eric Y. Yen, MD, MS, MPH, University of California, Los Angeles. It was published online on May 7, 2026, in ACR Open Rheumatology.

The study relied on the quality and accuracy of death certificate data, which may have underreported lupus as a cause of death. Death certificate data for some causes, such as cardiovascular disease, may have been overestimated. Additionally, the NIH funding data used for comparison did not capture all sources of research support.

The study did not report any source of funding. The authors declared having no conflicts of interest.

Title: Vasectomy Reversal More Cost-Effective Than IVF
Description:
Marcia  Frellick

Vasectomy reversal is the most cost-effective strategy for couples post-vasectomy, particularly when the female partner is younger than 40 years, new research presented at the American Urological Association (AUA) 2026 Annual Meeting indicates.

Researchers led by Dhruv Puri, MD, a urology resident with UC San Diego Health, looked at the cost-effectiveness equation in the context of employers' caps on employees' fertility benefits.

More employers who offer the benefits are capping them, the researchers wrote in their abstract, leaving postvasectomy couples who want to conceive with the choices of microsurgical vasectomy reversal or direct testicular sperm extraction (TESA) with in vitro fertilization (IVF)/intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI).

Although assisted reproductive therapy (ART) options may bring similar or slightly higher efficiency in older partners, with reversal, “the majority of couples under 40 can achieve pregnancy within typical $30,000-$40,000 employer fertility benefits, supporting routine consideration of reversal as the preferred initial approach,” according to Puri and colleagues.

Procedure costs included vasectomy reversal ($3500-$17,000), TESA ($1000-$10,000), and ICSI ($14,500 per cycle). The team used employer fertility benefit caps of $30,000 and $40,000 to estimate out-of-pocket and employer-paid costs.

The researchers found that reversal vs TESA/ICSI achieved higher pregnancy rates (75.8% vs 67.3%) and lower average total costs ($32,114 vs $34,648). Among couples with female partners younger than 40 years, reversal resulted in pregnancy rates of 91.7% vs 83.2% for ART, with average costs of $25,414 and $31,098. For partners at least 40 years old, reversal achieved 38.2% vs 29.7% pregnancy rates at average costs of $47,960 and $43,043, so there was a marginal advantage for ART for couples in this group.

Under employer fertility caps, 50% of reversal couples and 35% of ART couples achieved pregnancy within a $30,000 benefit, increasing to 61% and 55%, respectively, with a $40,000 cap.

Vasectomy reversal traditionally is not covered by insurance, and other options also may not be covered. “A lot of our patients are paying cash for infertility treatment, and we have to counsel them appropriately,” Puri told Medscape Medical News.

He said prior research has shown that reversal is cost-effective, but “the surprise is more the magnitude. I think previous analyses haven't really valued how expensive IVF and ICSI really are.” Even if an employer is paying $30,000-$40,000 benefits for fertility, it still makes more sense to reverse and then start to go down the pathway of IVF, Puri said.

“With costs in general going up, we may see a shift in patients asking their physicians for options because they have limited resources,” said David Shin, MD, director of the Division of Sexual Health and Fertility at Hackensack Meridian Health Hackensack University Medical Center in Edison, New Jersey.

He told Medscape Medical News it's not surprising that reversal is the most cost-effective because if reversal is successful, then the couple may be able to have children naturally, “whereas if you're using ART you're going to have to do that every single time.”

Shin noted that time since the vasectomy may affect individual couples' choice of treatment because the more years since the surgery, the lower the success rate.

Puri said while they were not able to calculate individual values for each patient, such as time since vasectomy, the study uses a microsimulation model of 100,000 couples to calculate results.

He said a sensitivity analysis also tested whether taking all the probability analysis and costs and adding or subtracting 20% would produce the same result, and it did, further strengthening their conclusions.

Puri added that IVF and ICSI “put a lot of burden on your partner when you're thinking ovarian stimulation and egg retrieval going through cycle after cycle. So if we can reverse and increase the odds before going through that entire process, I think that's a really meaningful benefit.”

He also said he hopes this work emphasizes that urologists should be included in the beginning conversations with postvasectomy couples instead of going straight to IVF and ICSI and focusing on the female partner.

Puri and Shin reported having no relevant financial relationships.

Marcia Frellick is an independent, Chicago-based healthcare journalist and a regular contributor to Medscape Medical News.

Title: France Expands 2026 Vaccination Rules Amid Resurgence
Description:
Jean-Bernard  Gervais

In an opinion piece published in Le Parisien, a French daily newspaper, during European Immunization Week in April 2026, some 300 researchers, physicians, writers, journalists, leaders of scientific institutions, members of parliament, and civil society figures warn that what science has long established is now being challenged. They say childhood immunization programs that once protected millions are being abandoned and that the alleged link between vaccines and autism — a claim repeatedly and rigorously dismissed by the international scientific community — is being portrayed to the public as if it were credible. The main public health agency of the US, the CDC, in November 2025, briefly amplified a false theory about a vaccine-autism connection after a policy reversal pushed by Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr; physicians and scientific experts have since refuted that misinformation.

In the preamble to their piece, the authors remind readers that vaccination — first used against smallpox by Edward Jenner in 1796 — now prevents nearly 4 million deaths each year. They evoked the polio era: summer beaches closed, public pools drained, hospital wards full of patients in iron lungs, and mothers watching every stumble as if it might signal catastrophe. They recalled smallpox's horrors — ravaged faces, devastated villages and roughly 300 million deaths in the 20th century — and noted that in France diphtheria (then called “croup”) once suffocated infants in their cradles. Overall, they say vaccination has cut infant mortality by about 40% over the past 50 years, and COVID vaccination alone has saved an estimated 1.4 million lives. Despite these clear gains, voices are rising that deny vaccination's benefits — voices that do not speak in the name of science, but in the name of ideologies that manipulate fears. Voices that, for the first time in modern history, come from some of the most influential centers of power on the planet.

The authors warned that this scientific denialism has helped drive a documented resurgence of measles in multiple regions and a rebound in invasive meningococcal infections — trends that create a real risk for wider resurgence. They emphasized that vaccination is a collective responsibility and that it extends beyond humans: About 75% of emerging infectious diseases originate in animals; therefore, human health and animal health are inseparable, and vaccinating animals helps protect people. They call on health professionals — physicians, pharmacists, midwives, nurses, and veterinarians — to keep speaking for science: You are the face of trust for patients. Every vaccine given is an act of hope, every clear explanation is a victory against doubt, and every refutation of misleading arguments can persuade.

The French Ministry of Health, for its part, reminds the public on the occasion of European Immunization Week of the changes in the 2026 vaccination schedule:

Title: New Benchmark for Reduced-Intensity Sickle Cell BMT Set
Description:
M. Alexander  Otto, PA, MMSc

Johns Hopkins University investigators have reported a novel, reduced-intensity conditioning regimen for severe sickle cell disease (SCD) that allows bone marrow transplants in patients who cannot tolerate traditional myeloablative conditioning.

The regimen also appears to preserve fertility in some women and to improve upon an alternative reduced-intensity conditioning regimen first published in 2024 by limiting graft failure in children and shortening the duration of posttransplant immunosuppression.

The Hopkins approach combines antithymocyte globulin, fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, and single-fraction 400 cGy total body irradiation. Posttransplant graft-vs-host disease (GVHD) prophylaxis includes additional cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate mofetil, and sirolimus.

The investigators reported outcomes in 43 patients, 36 with haploidentical donors and seven with fully human leukocyte antigen-matched donors, in their paper that was recently published in Blood Advances. The median age was 23 years, with patients aged ranging from 5 to 41 years.

Disease-free survival (DFS) probability at 2 years was 94.5%, with only two graft failures. The 5-year overall survival probability was 95.5%, and the cumulative incidence of grade 3/4 acute GVHD was 2.4%.

There were no bone marrow transplant-related deaths. Among 27 female patients, 12 resumed menses and two had successful pregnancies. Of 21 patients who were using opioids before transplant, 67% had stopped by 12 months. The median hospital stay for adults was only 12 days.

Hopkins SCD transplant specialist Robert Brodsky, MD, senior investigator on the protocol, called the regimen a “game changer for sickle cell anemia” in a post on X.

In their report, he and his colleagues explained that their protocol offers low toxicity, provides durable engraftment, and preserves fertility for some patients, all “with comparable DFS to matched related myeloablative [bone marrow transplant]…and no differences in engraftment between children and adults.”

In short, the regimen expands “access to curative therapy,” they concluded.

Asked for comment, Vanderbilt University sickle cell specialist Michael DeBaun, the senior developer of the earlier 2024 reduced-intensity conditioning protocol, said the Hopkins approach “provides another opportunity for children and especially adults to be cured of sickle cell disease” without the risks for myeloablative therapy.

With Hopkins' publication, clinicians now have two reduced-intensity conditioning options to choose from in addition to three myeloablative options: traditional bone marrow transplant and the recently approved gene therapies exagamglogene autotemcel and lovotibeglogene autotemcel.

“The next critical question is which of these five curative options maximizes benefit and minimizes short- and long-term adverse health outcomes,” DeBaun said.

The Hopkins team made a strong case for their protocol, which Brodsky dubbed the “Baltimore regimen” in his post.

For one, many adults with SCD cannot tolerate myeloablative conditioning, thereby ruling out not only standard transplants but also both new gene therapies. In addition, the authors noted myeloablation carries a high risk for infertility, and the gene therapies are at least five times more expensive than bone marrow transplants.

The authors also noted potential advantages over the 2024 regimen, which adds thiotepa to the conditioning regimen and uses 200 cGY total body irradiation instead of 400 cGY. Posttransplant GVHD prophylaxis is the same.

In the 2024 report, patients stayed on immunosuppression for almost a year, whereas 10 patients at Hopkins were able to stop at 6 months. Also, there were eight graft failures with the earlier reduced-intensity conditioning protocol, all of them in children. In contrast, there was only one graft failure among the 16 children at Hopkins, “which is reassuring,” the investigators said.

Fertility preservation “is another major advantage,” they noted. Overall, 44% of female patients resumed menses, and among men with endocrine monitoring, five returned to normal hormone function after transplant.

Post-bone marrow transplant complications included viremia and bacterial infections that resolved with supportive care. There were no infection-related deaths or graft failures.

The Hopkins team noted that transplants should be done at an earlier age when appropriate because it improves survival and organ function recovery and reduces bone marrow transplant complications and subsequent healthcare use.

The work was funded by the National Cancer Institute and the Maryland Stem Cell Research Fund. The authors and DeBaun reported having no relevant disclosures.

M. Alexander Otto is a physician assistant with a master's degree in medical science and a journalism degree from Newhouse. He is an award-winning medical journalist who worked for several major news outlets before joining Medscape. Alex is also an MIT Knight Science Journalism fellow. Email: aotto@medscape.net

Title: Antibiotic Stewardship: Five Tips to Reduce Resistance
Description:
Michael van den Heuvel

Antibiotics are among the most effective treatments available in modern medicine. However, inappropriate prescriptions continue to accelerate antimicrobial resistance. Every decision to prescribe an antibiotic should be evidence-based and guided by established recommendations. Antibiotic stewardship programs have shown that structured prescribing approaches can improve the quality of care and clinical outcomes.

How can these principles be effectively applied in daily clinical practice?

Differentiating viral infections from bacterial infections is a major challenge in clinical practice. Particularly in respiratory tract infections diagnostic uncertainty, often leads clinicians to prescribe antibiotics “just to be on the safe side,” — even when patients are unlikely to benefit.

Targeted use of clinical scoring systems and biomarkers, such as procalcitonin, can help clinicians assess the likelihood of bacterial infection more accurately. Studies have shown that biomarker-guided strategies can significantly reduce antibiotic use without compromising patient safety.

Bottom line: Antibiotics should not be prescribed without clear evidence supporting bacterial infection.

Long-term antibiotic treatment courses are increasingly being questioned. Randomized studies involving conditions such as pneumonia, urinary tract infections, and intra-abdominal infections have shown that shorter treatment durations can achieve clinical outcomes similar to those observed with longer courses.

Shorter courses also reduce the risk for side effects, selection of resistant organisms, and microbiome disruption. Therefore, Guidelines increasingly recommend individualized treatment durations that are kept as short as clinically appropriate.

Bottom line: Treatment duration should be decided carefully and reassessed regularly rather than based on routine standard guidelines.

Choosing the appropriate antibiotic is critical both for the success of treatment and for preventing the selection of resistant organisms. However, broad-spectrum antibiotics continue to be used too often in routine clinical practice for patients who could be treated effectively with narrow-spectrum antibiotics.

Guideline-based decisions supported by local resistance data and microbiologic testing improve treatment outcomes. Early treatment adjustment based on pathogen detection (“targeted therapy”) remains a core principle of modern antibiotic strategies. New diagnostic methods, including molecular testing, support a faster transition to targeted therapy and help reduce the broad-spectrum use of antibiotics.

Bottom line: Rational and targeted antibiotic selection can reduce resistance and improve clinical outcomes.

Pressure from patients to meet treatment expectations is an important contributor to unnecessary antibiotic prescription and should not be underestimated. Physicians prescribe antibiotics significantly more often when they believe that patients expect them to do so.

Communication plays a crucial role in addressing this issue. Studies show that transparent discussions about the natural course of illness, the lack of benefit from antibiotics in viral infections, and potential adverse effects can significantly improve acceptance of antibiotic-free treatment strategies.

Approaches such as “delayed prescribing” may also help balance patient expectations with rational treatment decisions. In this approach, the antibiotic is not prescribed at once and is used only if the symptoms do not improve or worsen.

Bottom line: Patients should understand why antibiotics may be the wrong choice in certain situations, and physicians should avoid prescribing antibiotics because of perceived pressure from patient expectations.

Antibiotic treatment requires structured reassessment. Within 48-72 hours, initial microbiologic findings and clinical follow-up data are usually available and may support treatment adjustment. Delayed de-escalation is associated with higher mortality.

The goal is to de-escalate, narrow, or entirely discontinue treatment when antibiotics are no longer indicated. These so-called “antibiotic timeouts” are considered an important component of stewardship programs and have been shown to reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure.

Bottom line: Reassessment should be planned at the start of treatment.

Rational antibiotic prescription involves more than choosing the correct drug. Appropriate indication, treatment duration, antibiotic selection, and ongoing monitoring are crucial. Consistently applying these principles supports evidence-based, patient-centered treatment and makes an effective contribution to the fight against antibiotic resistance.

Title: Coffee Linked to Better Cognition in Patients With PD
Description:
Edited by Vineeta Teotia

Coffee consumption was linked to enhanced executive function compared to nonconsumption in patients with early Parkinson's disease (PD), a new study showed. However, smoking status showed no independent association with cognitive performance.

“Our findings suggest that while caffeine may correlate with certain cognitive benefits, these results require cautious interpretation and further validation in independent cohorts,” the investigators wrote.

This study was led by Hak-Loh Lee, MD, Chonnam National University Hospital, Gwangju, South Korea. It was published online on April 15 in Parkinsonism & Related Disorders.

The single-center design and clinic-based cohort may have limited generalizability to advanced or community-based populations. The history of consumption of coffee and smoking was based on interviews and may have led to recall errors and misclassification. Unmeasured factors such as depression, sleep disorders, physical activity, and medication effects may have influenced cognitive outcomes.

This study was funded by the Bio&Medical Technology Development Program of the National Research Foundation, which is funded by the Korean government. The investigators reported having no relevant conflicts of interest.

Title: Germany Flags Safety Risks in ‘Vitamin Drip' Trend
Description:
Michael van den Heuvel

High-dose infusions of vitamins, trace elements, or amino acids — once confined to medical care — have migrated into the lifestyle market under names like “drip spa,” “vitamin drip,” “NAD+ therapy,” and “longevity infusion.” Marketed with claims of more energy, better skin, faster recovery, and a stronger immune system, these services have prompted an explicit health-risk warning from Germany's Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (BfArM), the country's national drug regulator.

Unlike conventional medical infusion therapies, these offerings are aimed primarily at healthy people. Marketing focuses on wellness, performance, and longevity — promising more energy, improved concentration, faster recovery after exercise or stress, a stronger immune system, and better skin or overall well-being. Terms such as “healthy aging,” “biohacking,” and “detox” also feature prominently in promotional materials.

These services are widely promoted on platforms such as Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube, where influencers, fitness coaches, and self-styled longevity experts present infusion treatments as part of a modern health and lifestyle concept. Posts often show luxurious spa settings and custom “vitamin cocktails.” Such offers are now appearing in large German cities, often in beauty clinics, wellness studios, or private “longevity clinics.”

The market has been growing markedly for years. In the US, intravenous (IV) lounges and mobile infusion services are already established parts of the wellness industry, and similar developments are increasingly visible in Europe. Treatments often cost several hundred euros per session and are marketed as premium health products.

The downside is thatthe scientific evidence is thin. Several review articles conclude that for most of the claimed wellness or longevity effects, there is no robust evidence. A 2025 review explicitly describes the study situation as insufficient and notes that high-quality clinical data are especially lacking for healthy people.

There are individual clinical studies of high-dose IV vitamin C, for example, in patients with cancer or critically ill patients, but those results cannot be generalized to healthy people. Some studies also point to potential harms: In one randomized trial of IV vitamin C in patients with sepsis, treatment was associated with a higher risk for death or persistent organ dysfunction.

Thus, the scientific basis for many wellness infusions remains weak. While infusion therapies are indispensable in medicine for clearly defined indications — for example, in deficiency states, malabsorption or certain cancer therapies — there is currently little robust evidence supporting their use in healthy people.

BfArM warns that these wellness infusions are not equivalent to routinely approved medicinal products. Manufacturers of approved infusion therapies must demonstrate efficacy, quality, and safety — evidence that is currently missing for many commercial “wellness” infusions.

According to BfArM, drip-spa infusions can cause various health risks, including hypervitaminosis due to overdosing, fluid overload, electrolyte and acid-base imbalances, and allergic reactions up to anaphylactic shock. Also possible are complications typical of IV procedures, such as extravasation, air embolism, or circulatory problems resulting from overly rapid fluid administration.

Vulnerable patient groups are particularly at risk — for example, older adults and people with heart, kidney, or metabolic diseases.

As demand for so-called longevity infusions is likely to continue rising amid the overlap of preventive medicine, lifestyle medicine and social-media marketing, it is all the more important to provide an evidence-based assessment of the promised benefits and potential risks. The medical character of IV treatments gives many patients the impression of scientific legitimacy, even though reliable data are lacking.

Title: Ketamine Tied to Rapid Relief of Depression Symptoms, Suicidal Ideation
Description:
Carla  Cantor

Treatment with intravenous (IV) ketamine was associated with rapid relief for adults with major depressive disorder (MDD), providing significant reductions in suicidal thoughts and depressive symptoms within hours of a single infusion, a systematic review and meta-analysis found.

The study, which analyzed 26 randomized clinical trials involving 1166 patients, updated evidence for ketamine as a fast-acting intervention in depression by incorporating more recent trials, including studies of repeated infusions.

The speed of the antisuicidal effect could make IV ketamine a potential option in emergency treatment for patients at high risk for suicidal behavior, the researchers noted.

“There is an urgent need for more effective interventions that rapidly reduce depressive symptoms and suicide risk,” corresponding author Taeho Greg Rhee, PhD, of the University of Connecticut School of Medicine, Farmington, Connecticut, and colleagues wrote.

The study was published online on May 6 in JAMA Psychiatry.

MDD remains a leading cause of disability globally, affecting an estimated 280 million people. Treatment resistance, commonly defined as failure to respond to at least two antidepressant trials, remains a major challenge. Patients with treatment-resistant depression (TRD) have a higher risk for suicidal ideation and suicide attempts and deaths, the authors noted.

Ketamine, a noncompetitive antagonist of the N-methyl-D-aspartate glutamate receptor, has drawn increasing interest as a rapidly acting antidepressant and potential treatment for patients at risk for suicidal behavior. While IV ketamine is not approved by the FDA for psychiatric illness, it is increasingly used off label for these conditions.

Intranasal esketamine is FDA-approved for TRD and for depressive symptoms in adults with MDD with acute suicidal ideation or behavior.

In March 2026, IV racemic ketamine received the first regulatory approval in the history for treatment of adults with severe suicidal symptoms from the French drug regulatory agency.

For the current study, investigators included 626 patients who received IV ketamine and 540 who received saline or midazolam (control group). Most studies used ketamine at a dose of 0.5 mg/kg.

The included trials enrolled patients with MDD, TRD, bipolar depression, and severe suicidal ideation. In preliminary analyses, however, the authors found that depression type may influence response, with the strongest antidepressant effects seen in unipolar depression.

In the meta-analysis, a single ketamine infusion was associated with significantly lower suicidal symptoms at 24 hours compared with control treatment, the study's main acute timepoint (standardized mean difference [SMD], -0.69; 95% CI, -0.98 to -0.40). Suicidal symptoms were also lower at 1 month after a single infusion, and repeated infusions reduced suicidal symptoms at the end of the treatment.

Depressive symptoms improved even earlier. Ketamine was associated with significant reductions in depressive symptoms 4 hours after a single infusion compared with control treatment (SMD, -1.74; 95% CI, -2.43 to -1.06), with benefits also seen at 24 hours, 3 days, and 1 week. Repeated infusions reduced depressive symptoms at the end of the treatment.

In the ketamine group, increased depressive symptom response rates were reported at 4 hours, 24 hours, 3 days, and 1 week after a single infusion compared to the control group. Remission rates, however, did not differ significantly between groups.

The response-remission distinction suggests that ketamine may rapidly reduce symptom burden without necessarily producing full remission during the short follow-up periods studied, researchers noted.

Ketamine was associated with adverse effects, although the majority were transient and resolved within hours of infusion, the study showed. These included headache, dissociation, nausea, dizziness, temporary blood pressure or cardiorespiratory effects, numbness or tingling, and visual disturbances.

Among the studies that reported serious adverse events, the most common were hospitalizations, suicide attempts, suicide, nonsuicide-related deaths, and other medical conditions. The majority of these were unrelated to treatment, researchers wrote.

The authors acknowledged several limitations. The included trials varied in design, follow-up duration, patient population, comparator, dosing schedule, and outcome measures. Some trials were small, limiting statistical power. Ketamine's dissociative effects also could have compromised blinding in some studies, introducing potential bias.

Longer-term outcomes also remained uncertain. The authors noted that safety data were largely limited to the acute phase of treatment, and that questions remain about relapse risk, maintenance strategies, and potential long-term safety concerns.

“Given the typically long duration of depressive illness, longer-term outcomes are needed,” the authors wrote.

The study was supported by the Intramural Research Program at the National Institute of Mental Health, the Canadian Institutes of Health Research, the AFP Innovation Fund, the Patient-Centered Outcomes Research Institute, and several National Institutes of Health grants. Disclosure information for study authors is available in the original publication.

Title: Hydronephrosis on USG Predicts Stones but May Not Cut CT Use
Description:
Edited by Anushree Chaphalkar



Among emergency department (ED) patients with suspected renal colic who underwent renal point-of-care ultrasound (rPOCUS), hydronephrosis predicted kidney stones and early urologic intervention and was linked to increased CT use and similar length of stay (LOS) in the ED.

"Hydronephrosis identified on rPOCUS was associated with ureteral stone presence and early urologic intervention among ED patients with suspected renal colic. Notably, hydronephrosis on rPOCUS was associated with increased, rather than decreased, CT utilization in this cohort," the authors wrote.

The study was led by Mark D. Scheatzle, MD, MPH, Allegheny General Hospital, Pittsburgh. It was published online on April 12, 2026, in The Journal of Emergency Medicine.

The single-center retrospective design of the study limited the generalizability of the findings to settings with different ultrasound expertise and workflows. The study was also subject to potential selection bias due to clinician-directed rPOCUS use, confounding by indication in CT ordering, outcome assessment limited to the CT subgroup, possible misclassification from unverified rPOCUS interpretations, and unmeasured system factors such as crowding and staffing that may have affected the ED LOS.

The study received no funding. The authors reported having no competing interests.

Title: Isotretinoin Plus Ablative Laser Safe, Effective for Rhinophyma
Description:
Deborah  Brauser

SAVANNAH, Ga. — Combining carbon dioxide (CO2) laser resurfacing with isotretinoin is both safe and effective for treating rhinophyma in patients with rosacea, a new study suggests.

Rhinophyma is a severe, advanced form of rosacea marked by the enlargement of sebaceous glands within the nasal tissue, making the nose appear bumpy, red, and bulbous.

In a retrospective review of 15 adults with the condition, fully ablative CO2 laser therapy plus low-dose perioperative oral isotretinoin was associated with posttreatment improvement on photographic assessments by blinded reviewers, no delayed re-epithelialization or abnormal scarring, low recurrence, and high patient satisfaction.

Study investigator Isabella Camacho-Hubbard, MD, Dermatology Laser & Vein Specialists of the Carolinas, Charlotte, North Carolina, noted that the results were gratifying, especially in this particular patient population.

“ This condition can be disfiguring, and it can affect mental health. Although this isn't an easy treatment per se, and the recovery time can be long, it's worth it in the end,” Camacho-Hubbard told Medscape Medical News. “I tell patients: It's a journey but we'll get through it together,” she added.

The findings were reported on May 9 at The American Society for Laser Medicine and Surgery (ASLMS) 2026 Annual Meeting.

With rhinophyma, hypertrophy of nasal sebaceous glands causes disruption of an individual's nasal architecture.

Oral isotretinoin monotherapy has been shown to be effective for managing the condition, primarily by reducing the size and growth of the sebaceous glands. Ablative resurfacing lasers have also proved useful to “recreate a natural nasal contour,” the investigators noted in their abstract.

Combining the two modalities to improve outcomes, however, has been considered risky. In fact, there was a call to stop use of isotretinoin 6-12 months before surgical and ablative laser procedures because of the potential risk for delays in wound healing.

Although recent research has challenged those concerns, “there are no studies to our knowledge” that have demonstrated the safety of fully ablative CO2 laser treatment plus oral isotretinoin for rhinophyma, the researchers reported.

The current single-center, retrospective review was conducted from 2017 to 2025 and included 14 men and one women aged 56-85 years (Fitzpatrick I-V skin types) with rhinophyma and a history of having undergone at least one prior CO2 laser treatment.

All participants underwent fully ablative CO2 resurfacing with a Lumenis Ultrapulse laser with a 1.3 mm collimated spot scanner. Additionally, “the focused fractional 120 µm microscanner was used on 93% of patients,” the researchers noted.

“Total depth of ablation was between 0.5-1.5 mm in most cases, with the goal to reach the depth of the sebaceous glands and observe visible contour improvement,” they added.

Five patients also initiated oral isotretinoin within 2 months prior to resurfacing, two patients initiated it during an inter-procedural period, and eight patients initiated it within 1 month posttreatment. Doses of isotretinoin ranged from 0.27 to 0.57 mg/kg (mean, 0.35 mg/kg).

The primary outcome was safety. Secondary outcomes included recurrence rate, breathing improvement, quality of life, patient satisfaction, and treatment recommendation, assessed with a five-question survey administered by phone.

Facial images were also taken at baseline (before treatment) and at the most recent follow-up. The photos were presented in a randomized order to three blinded reviewers (two dermatologists and one plastic surgeon), who classified whether the images were taken pre- or post-operatively. After the reviewers were told the correct status, they provided rhinophyma severity index (RHIS) scores for each postoperative image.

In survey results, 100% of the respondents rated their cosmetic satisfaction as “good” or “excellent,” 100% said they would recommend the treatment to others, 92% reported no rhinophyma recurrence and rated their quality of life as improved or significantly improved, and 60% reported improvement in breathing. The one participant who reported recurrence rated it as “minimal.”

In the image analysis, the reviewers correctly identified which photographs were taken pre- and post-operatively in all cases. Change scores on the RHIS ranged from -5 to 1 points (mean, -2.02 points), with negative scores representing improvement in severity.

“The findings demonstrated improvement on photographic assessment despite inter-reviewer variability,” said Camacho-Hubbard, who then showed photos showing dramatic changes to the audience.

Among the 73% of participants who had at least 6-month follow-up data, milia developed in 25%, and mild hypergranulation in 13%. However, the latter completely resolved after use of topical silver nitrate, the investigators reported.

There were no incidents of delayed re-epithelialization, infection, persistent erythema, keloids, dyspigmentation, or scarring.

Asked afterwards if she was surprised at how positive the results were, Camacho-Hubbard said, they were “amazing and absolutely life-changing” for patients and added that it's one of her favorite procedures to perform.

“It's very rewarding. Some patients may not be aware that this treatment is possible, so it would be great to get more information out there to the public that it is possible to successfully treat rhinophyma,” Camacho-Hubbard said.

“Adding of low-dose isotretinoin with fully ablated CO2 laser really does make a big difference in decreasing recurrence and providing that overall textural improvement,” she noted.

However, “further prospective, well-controlled studies are still needed,” Camacho-Hubbard added.

“They showed very impressive results, and the before and after pictures were very nice,” Jeremy A. Brauer, MD, director of Spectrum Skin and Laser, New York City, told Medscape Medical News when asked for comment on the study. He was a co-moderator of the session where the results were presented.

The other co-moderator Chris Robb, MD, PhD, co-founder of the Skin & Allergy Center, Tennessee, agreed with Brauer. “It's also nice because it gives us a little more insight into the constant argument about when you can and cannot use isotretinoin with ablative laser,” he said.

Robb added that in the past, some members of the field suggested waiting a year before adding the second modality, with others suggested waiting 6 months or until s ebaceous gland activity resumes. “But in this case, sebaceous gland activity is actually the problem. So that might be why isotretinoin was beneficial with CO2,” he said.

Neither Robb nor Brauer was involved with the research. Robb noted that he was surprised to see this combination used for rhinophyma in the study.

“Conventionally, we've all been trained not to do this; but I'm really impressed with the results,” he said.

Camacho-Hubbard, Brauer, and Robb reported having no relevant financial relationships.

Title: Undiagnosed Dementia Tied to Increased Mortality Risk
Description:
Edited by Vineeta Teotia

The proportion of dementia cases that were undiagnosed was high in the US and Brazil in a new cohort study, but the percentage was markedly higher in Brazil (76% vs 45%). Undiagnosed dementia was also associated with increased 4-year mortality compared to no dementia in both countries.

“Undiagnosed dementia is common and associated with increased mortality, identifying a vulnerable population missed by current diagnostic pathways,” the investigators wrote.

“As global populations age and the number of people living with dementia increases, these cross-national differences support efforts to improve timely, equitable detection and access to postdiagnostic care, particularly in lower-resource settings,” they added.

This study was led by Marlon Juliano Romero Aliberti, MD, PhD, Hospital das Clinicas HCFMUSP, Universidade de Sao Paulo, São Paulo, Brazil. It was published online on April 29 in Alzheimer's & Dementia.

Comparison across countries may have been affected by differences in measurement and healthcare, including variations in how reported diagnoses were ascertained across cohorts. The dementia classification relied on validated survey algorithms based on cognitive and functional performance rather than clinical diagnosis. The cognitive battery did not capture all domains, particularly visuospatial and executive function, and the study lacked linkage to administrative health records. Additionally, self- or proxy-reported diagnosis of dementia probably reflected limited awareness rather than the absence of a diagnosis.

This study was funded by the Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de, São Paulo, Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico, the National Institute on Aging, and the Alzheimer's Association. The investigators reported having no relevant conflicts of interest.
